does: This paper develops Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior (FedVPA-GP), a framework that enables decentralized alignment of large language models while preserving heterogeneous user preferences and privacy. The core contribution addresses posterior collapse in variational preference learning by introducing a Federated Mixture Prior that allows clients to use aggregate population statistics as a dynamic prior, combined with an Orthogonal Loss that explicitly separates preference prototypes in the latent space. The method operates in a federated setting where each client maintains local preference distributions without sharing raw data, and experiments on the HH-RLHF dataset demonstrate that FedVPA-GP outperforms monolithic reward models that average conflicting preferences, achieving better personalization across diverse user groups.
connects: This work builds on Variational Preference Learning, which models diverse human preferences as distinct distributions in latent space, addressing limitations of single reward models that cannot capture conflicting objectives like helpfulness versus harmlessness. It extends federated learning frameworks for LLM alignment, which typically enforce shared reward models that suppress preference diversity. The paper directly responds to the posterior collapse problem endemic to variational inference under local data scarcity and heterogeneity—a challenge well-established in distributed generative modeling. By incorporating Gumbel-Softmax parameterization and mixture priors, it adapts techniques from federated Bayesian inference and variational autoencoders to the preference learning domain, building on prior work in privacy-preserving personalization for language models.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward privacy-preserving personalized LLMs that respect user preference heterogeneity at scale. It enables future work in federated preference discovery, where different user communities can maintain distinct value alignments without central aggregation or homogenization. The approach could support applications in multi-stakeholder AI systems where conflicting objectives (safety vs. helpfulness, different cultural values) need coexistence rather than compromise. Follow-up directions include extending to hierarchical preference structures, adaptive mixture weighting across federated clients, theoretical analysis of convergence under non-identifiable preference components, and applications to other decentralized alignment challenges like constitutional AI in federated settings.
